title: Persistent Connections, Pipelining, Ordering, Reuse, and Closure · Failure Repair Bay
summary: An incident engineer receives a plausible but defective persistent-connection request and response state machine; retain the raw transcript, isolate the first divergent state, and preserve a regression corpus entry. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in persistent-connection request and response state machine and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence…
objectives: Determine HTTP/1.0 and HTTP/1.1 persistence from version, Connection options, framing completeness, errors, and local policy.
Consume, reject-and-close, or deliberately drain the current framed request before parsing a subsequent request.
Preserve request-response ordering across pipelined HTTP/1.1 messages even when application work finishes out of order.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Inspect the failure record · Debug Persistence Defaults
steps.instruction:
debug case — A debug protocol team handles case 4534 in a multi-tenant intake service near its connection and memory ceilings. Build deterministic Go evidence to preserve HTTP/1.1 response order and reject reuse after incomplete or ambiguous input; preserve this operating constraint: one parser state transition may consume bytes only after its full precondition holds; then reject a protocol transition while optimistic bytes are buffered and prevent desynchronization. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Determine HTTP/1.0 and HTTP/1.1 persistence from version, Connection options, framing completeness, errors, and local policy.

Inspect the Persistence Defaults trace for this debug case. Which record connects a precondition, observed state change, and boundary result?

Evidence target: Inspect the failure record · Debug Persistence Defaults.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for determine http/1.0 and http/1.1 persistence from version, connection options, framing completeness, errors, and local policy. For Inspect the failure record · Debug Persistence Defaults, keep Persistence Defaults connected to executable Go behavior evidence.

steps.title: Implement the cause-level repair · Debug Desynchronization Close
steps.instruction:

Browser code must not open sockets or listeners, negotiate TLS, contact networks or proxies, capture packets, read host state, or use credentials; verify those boundaries later with the named Go 1.26 protocol transfer gates.

Build deterministic Go evidence to preserve HTTP/1.1 response order and reject reuse after incomplete or ambiguous input; preserve this operating constraint: all attacker-controlled counts must be validated before allocation or slicing; then change certificate identity while ALPN stays valid and reject the connection. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Close and discard connection state after ambiguous framing, parse failure, incomplete output, unexpected residual bytes, or state disagreement.

For Publish the incident record · Debug Desynchronization Close, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: Returning 400 is enough to resynchronize a persistent connection after malformed framing.
